g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Ambiguity in Grammars and Languages, a fundamental concept in automata theory.
g.text: Understanding Grammar Ambiguity | university 2022 Scheme | CSE

g.data-narration: A grammar is ambiguous when a single string can be derived in multiple different ways, creating confusion in parsing.
g.text: if there exists w in L(G) with
of the GRAMMAR, not the language

g.data-narration: Understanding ambiguity is crucial for compiler design because parsers need deterministic interpretation of code.
g.text: Compilers cannot deterministically parse ambiguous grammars
Multiple interpretations lead to inconsistent program behavior
Security vulnerabilities can arise from ambiguous syntax

g.data-narration: Formally, a context-free grammar G equals V, Sigma, P, S is ambiguous if there exists a string with multiple parse trees.
g.text: A CFG G = (V, Sigma, P, S) is ambiguous if:
exists w in L(G) with 2+ distinct parse trees
Language L is inherently ambiguous if:
for all G generating L, G is ambiguous

g.data-narration: Consider this classic ambiguous grammar for arithmetic expressions. The string id plus id times id has two valid derivations.
g.text: E arrow E + E vertical E * E vertical (E) vertical id

g.data-narration: These two parse trees show different operator precedence interpretations, demonstrating how ambiguity leads to different meanings.
g.text: Two Different Parse Trees for id + id * id
E arrow E + E arrow id + E*E
E arrow E * E arrow E + E * E

g.data-narration: The dangling else problem in programming languages creates ambiguity because the else clause can bind to different if statements.
g.text: S arrow if E then S
vertical if E then S else S
String: if E1 then if E2 then S1 else S2

g.data-narration: Some languages are inherently ambiguous, meaning no unambiguous grammar can generate them. This was proven by Rabin and Ullman in 1967.
g.text: if EVERY grammar generating L is ambiguous.
L = {a n b n c m d m : n,m >= 1} union {a n b m c m d n : n,m >= 1}

g.data-narration: When n equals m, strings like aabbccdd belong to both language subsets, requiring different parse trees for each derivation.
g.text: Why This Language is Inherently Ambiguous
belongs to BOTH sets, needs 2 derivations!
Set 2: aabbccdd arrow n=2, m=2 (different parse)

g.data-narration: To convert ambiguous to unambiguous grammars, introduce separate non-terminals for each precedence level to enforce operator hierarchy.
g.text: E arrow E + E vertical E * E vertical (E) vertical id
E arrow E + T vertical T
T arrow T * F vertical F

g.data-narration: By introducing separate non-terminals E, T, and F for different precedence levels, we enforce multiplication has higher precedence than addition.
g.text: Precedence: F (highest) arrow T arrow E (lowest) | Association: left-to-right

g.data-narration: Testing for ambiguity is undecidable in general, but practical heuristics include checking for nullable symbols and common prefixes.
Key points to remember: ambiguity is grammar-specific, left recursion is not the same as ambiguity, and empty productions often cause ambiguity.

g.text: Ambiguity is property of GRAMMAR, not language
Left recursion is not the same as Ambiguity

g.data-narration: Remember: focus on common examples like arithmetic expressions and if-else, and practice converting ambiguous to unambiguous grammars.

g.text: example (E arrow E+E vertical E*E vertical id)
